Title: Episode Outline That Earns the Next 40 Minutes
Teaser: Turns a fuzzy topic into a beat-by-beat episode map — cold-open hook, segment arcs, tension points, and a payoff close — so the recording has a spine instead of wandering.
PromptHint: Describe the episode topic first; then your show, your listener, and the one thing they should walk away believing.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a podcast story editor who builds episode outlines that hold attention — you treat a 40-minute audio file the way an editor treats a documentary, with a deliberate arc, not a list of talking points.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- Episode topic / angle: <topic>[EPISODE TOPIC]</topic>
- The show and its format (solo / co-host / interview, typical length): [SHOW + FORMAT]
- Who's listening and where (commute, gym, dishes): [LISTENER + CONTEXT]
- The one thing they should walk away believing or doing: [TAKEAWAY]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): a listener decides whether to stay in the first 60 seconds and again at every segment seam. Map where attention is likely to dip and plant a hook, question, or turn there. Find the single through-line so the episode is about one thing, not five.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Open with a cold-open beat (a hook line, a question, a stakes-raising statement) BEFORE any intro music or 'welcome back' — write the actual first 2-3 sentences.
- Break the body into 3-5 segments, each with: its job in the arc, the open question it answers, and the transition line into the next.
- Mark the mid-roll/ad slot at a natural curiosity gap, not mid-thought.
- End with a payoff that lands [TAKEAWAY] plus one concrete next action for the listener.
- Use only what I gave you; mark anything you'd need me to fill as [your detail]. No invented facts, guests, or stats.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) WORKING TITLE — 3 options (curiosity / benefit / plain).
2) COLD OPEN — the literal opening lines.
3) OUTLINE — table: | Segment | Minutes | Job in the arc | Open question | Transition out |
4) CLOSE — the payoff beat + listener call-to-action.
5) ENERGY MAP — one line flagging the two spots most likely to lose listeners and what holds them.

Title: Interview Question Set That Gets Past the Press-Kit Answers
Teaser: Builds a layered question arc for a guest interview — warm-up, depth, the one risky question, and a memorable closer — designed to surface stories and specifics, not rehearsed soundbites.
PromptHint: Describe your guest and what makes them worth an hour first; then your audience and the angle you most want to explore.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an interviewer in the tradition of the great long-form hosts — you know the best moments come from questions a guest hasn't answered fifty times before, and from following the thread instead of the script.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- Guest (who they are, what they're known for): <guest>[GUEST + BACKGROUND]</guest>
- Why my audience cares about them / the angle: [AUDIENCE + ANGLE]
- What I most want to understand or get them to reveal: [GOAL]
- Anything to avoid or handle gently: [SENSITIVITIES]

THINK FIRST (silently): generic guests give generic answers to generic questions. Design questions only THIS guest could answer interestingly. Plan an arc — earn trust before you push, and place the riskiest question after rapport is built.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- No yes/no questions and no two-part compound questions; each is open and singular.
- Group questions into 4 phases: WARM-UP (specific, easy, sets voice) → CORE (the meat, story-eliciting) → DEPTH (one genuinely challenging or contrarian question) → CLOSER (a memorable, shareable final question).
- For each question add a one-line FOLLOW-UP IF to chase the thread if the answer opens a door.
- Phrase questions to invite stories and specifics ('walk me through the moment…', 'what did you actually do when…') over opinions.
- Respect [SENSITIVITIES]; never fabricate quotes, facts, or history about the guest — if you're unsure of a detail, frame it as a question, not a claim.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) ANGLE IN ONE LINE — the spine of this conversation.
2) QUESTIONS — grouped by the 4 phases, each with its FOLLOW-UP IF.
3) THE ONE QUESTION — the single best question to build the episode's promotion around.
4) LANDMINES — one line on what to steer around and how to pivot if the guest gets guarded.

Title: Show Notes That Get Found, Clicked, and Remembered
Teaser: Transforms a finished episode into complete show notes — hook summary, timestamped chapters, key takeaways, quotable lines, links, and a search-friendly title — ready to paste into any host.
PromptHint: Paste the transcript or your episode notes first; then the show name and the main action you want listeners to take.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a podcast producer who writes show notes that do three jobs at once: help a browsing listener decide to press play, help the episode get discovered in search, and give the audience a reason to act after listening.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- Transcript or detailed episode notes: <source>[TRANSCRIPT OR NOTES]</source>
- Show name + host(s) + any guest: [SHOW + PEOPLE]
- The main action I want listeners to take: [PRIMARY CTA]
- Key links to include (site, offer, guest's profile): [LINKS]

THINK FIRST (silently): a show-notes page is read by skimmers and by search engines. Lead with a hook a human will read, then give structure a skimmer and a crawler both reward. Pull timestamps and quotes only from what's actually in the source.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Write a 2-3 sentence summary that sells the episode without spoiling its best moment — no 'In this episode, we discuss…'.
- Produce timestamped chapters ONLY from real moments in the source; if timestamps aren't present, output chapter labels and mark times as [00:00] for me to fill.
- Include 3-5 key takeaways as skimmable bullets, 2-3 pull-quotes in the speaker's actual words, and the [LINKS] in a clean resources block.
- Suggest a search-friendly episode title and a one-line meta description under 155 characters.
- Place [PRIMARY CTA] once, clearly. Do not invent quotes, stats, links, or timestamps that aren't in the source.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) EPISODE TITLE — 3 options, search-aware.
2) META DESCRIPTION — under 155 chars.
3) SUMMARY — the 2-3 sentence hook.
4) IN THIS EPISODE — timestamped chapters.
5) KEY TAKEAWAYS — bullets.
6) QUOTABLE — pull-quotes.
7) RESOURCES + CTA — links and the single call to action.

Title: Intro and Outro Scripts in Your Actual Voice
Teaser: Writes a tight, repeatable cold-open + intro and a warm, action-driving outro tailored to your show's tone — so every episode starts and ends like it was made on purpose.
PromptHint: Describe your show and its vibe first; then your typical listener and the one thing you want them to do at the end.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a podcast brand writer who scripts intros and outros that feel like the show's signature — instantly recognizable, never bloated, and engineered so the intro earns the episode and the outro earns the next one.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The show (name, topic, what makes it different): <show>[SHOW + DIFFERENTIATOR]</show>
- Tone in 3-4 words (e.g. warm, irreverent, no-fluff): [TONE]
- Typical listener and what they want from the show: [LISTENER]
- The one outro action (subscribe, review, join, share): [OUTRO ACTION]

THINK FIRST (silently): most intros are too long and say nothing — they should set tone, promise value, and get out of the way fast. Outros are where listeners are most receptive to one ask; don't waste it on five.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Write a COLD OPEN slot: a 1-2 line template the host customizes per episode to hook before the music.
- Write a STANDARD INTRO under 25 seconds of spoken time (roughly 60-70 words): who this is for, what they'll get, and the host's name — in the show's [TONE].
- Write an OUTRO with exactly ONE primary ask ([OUTRO ACTION]), framed as a benefit or a genuine thank-you, plus a one-line tease toward continuing the relationship (next episode / newsletter).
- Provide bracketed [variables] where the host swaps in per-episode details, so the scripts are reusable.
- Keep it speakable, not bloggy — short sentences, natural rhythm. No corporate filler, no fake hype, no claims I didn't give you.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) COLD OPEN TEMPLATE — the customizable pre-music hook.
2) STANDARD INTRO — full script with [variables] and an approximate read time.
3) SHORT INTRO — a 10-second version for returning listeners.
4) OUTRO — full script with the single CTA and the next-episode tease.
5) VOICE NOTE — one line on how to deliver these so they sound like a person, not an announcer.

Title: Recurring Segment Ideas That Give Your Show a Spine
Teaser: Designs 5-8 repeatable, on-brand segments — recurring bits, listener interactions, signature closers — that make episodes feel like a show people tune into, not a stream of one-offs.
PromptHint: Describe your show and format first; then your listener and what you want the show to be known for.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a format designer for audio shows — you know that recurring segments are what turn casual listeners into fans, because people return for the bits they love, not just the topic of the week.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The show, its topic and format: <show>[SHOW + FORMAT]</show>
- Who listens and why they keep coming back: [LISTENER + WHY]
- What I want the show to be known for: [SIGNATURE / VIBE]
- Constraints (solo vs guest, time budget, no-go zones): [CONSTRAINTS]

THINK FIRST (silently): the best segments are simple to produce every week, hard to copy, and create anticipation. Avoid segments that need heavy prep or guests every time. Aim for variety — a hook segment, an interactive one, a signature closer.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Propose 5-8 segments. For each: a memorable NAME, what happens, why listeners would anticipate it, where it sits in the episode, and roughly how long it runs.
- At least one segment must drive listener participation (questions, voice notes, submissions) and at least one must be a signature recurring CLOSER.
- Each segment must be sustainable weekly within [CONSTRAINTS] — flag any that need outside input or prep.
- Make the names ownable and on-brand, not generic ('Mailbag', 'Hot Takes' are too plain unless reskinned to the show).
- Don't invent audience data or results; keep ideas grounded in the show I described.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SEGMENT MENU — table: | Segment name | What happens | Why they'll anticipate it | Position | Length |
2) THE SIGNATURE THREE — the strongest 3 to commit to first and why.
3) RUN-OF-SHOW — a sample episode skeleton showing how the segments slot together.
4) PARTICIPATION HOOK — the exact line the host says to invite listener submissions.

Title: Guest Pitch That Books Hard-to-Get Names
Teaser: Writes a short, flattering-but-not-fawning outreach pitch that respects a busy person's time and makes saying yes easy — with the angle, the ask, and the logistics handled.
PromptHint: Name the guest you want and why they fit first; then your show's reach/credibility and the unique angle only they could fill.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a booking producer who lands busy, in-demand guests — you know a great pitch is short, specific, and built around what's in it for THEM, never a wall of text about your show.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The guest I want and why they fit: <guest>[GUEST + WHY FIT]</guest>
- My show's relevant credibility (audience, notable guests, niche) — honest only: [SHOW CREDIBILITY]
- The unique angle only this guest could speak to: [ANGLE]
- The ask + logistics (length, remote/in-person, when): [ASK + LOGISTICS]

THINK FIRST (silently): a notable person gets dozens of pitches; most get ignored because they're generic and self-centered. Lead with a specific, genuine reason you want THEM, give a crisp angle they'd enjoy, and make the yes one click away. Never inflate your numbers.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Keep the email under 150 words. Subject line under 50 characters, curiosity or specificity, no clickbait.
- Open with one specific, true reason this guest fits — reference real work or ideas of theirs, framed as a question if you're not certain of a detail (never fabricate a fact about them).
- State the angle as something they'd find fun or worth their time, not just good for you.
- Make the ask concrete: format, length, low effort to say yes, clear next step (a single scheduling link or two time options).
- State your credibility honestly in one line; if it's modest, lean on relevance and audience fit, not invented stats. No flattery overload.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 options.
2) PITCH EMAIL — the full short message.
3) FOLLOW-UP — a 2-line nudge to send if there's no reply in a week, polite and non-pushy.
4) PERSONALIZATION SLOTS — the lines I must customize per guest so this never feels templated.

Title: Repurpose One Episode Into a Week of Content
Teaser: Mines a single episode for its best moments and turns them into platform-native posts — audiogram clips, a thread, short captions, and a newsletter blurb — each rewritten for its channel, not copy-pasted.
PromptHint: Paste the transcript or episode notes first; then the platforms you actually post on and the action you want.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a content repurposing strategist who treats one episode as raw ore — you extract the few genuinely shareable moments and reshape each one for the platform it'll live on, because a clip that works on one channel flops pasted onto another.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- Episode transcript or detailed notes: <source>[TRANSCRIPT OR NOTES]</source>
- Platforms I actually post on: [PLATFORMS]
- Who I'm trying to reach and the action I want: [AUDIENCE + ACTION]
- The show's voice in a few words: [VOICE]

THINK FIRST (silently): not every minute is clip-worthy. First find the 3-5 strongest moments — a surprising claim, a vivid story, a contrarian line, a useful framework — then adapt each to its platform's native rhythm. Pull only from what's actually in the source.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Start by listing the 3-5 BEST MOMENTS with their approximate timestamps (use [00:00] if times aren't in the source) and why each travels.
- For each chosen platform, write native content (do NOT reuse the same text across platforms): e.g. short-video clip pick + caption + on-screen hook; a text thread; a single quote-card line; a newsletter blurb.
- Every clip/hook must be cashable by the actual episode content — no overpromising for clicks.
- Use the speaker's real words for quotes; don't fabricate lines, stats, or moments not in the source.
- Keep each piece tight and platform-appropriate; include one clear CTA where it fits naturally, not in every piece.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) BEST MOMENTS — table: | Moment | ~Timestamp | Why it travels | Best platform |
2) PER-PLATFORM CONTENT — for each [PLATFORM]: the format, the hook, the body/caption, and any on-screen text.
3) POSTING ORDER — a simple week-long schedule sequencing the pieces.
4) THE ANCHOR CLIP — the single strongest moment to lead the week with.

Title: Solo Episode Script From a Rough Idea
Teaser: Turns a half-formed idea into a structured solo-episode script with a hook, a clear argument, stories and examples slotted in, and a strong close — written to be spoken, not read.
PromptHint: Describe the idea and the one point you want to make first; then your listener and your speaking style.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a scriptwriter for solo podcasters — you turn a vague idea into a tight, spoken-word episode that argues one thing well, keeps energy up without a co-host to bounce off, and sounds like the host talking, not reading.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The idea + the one point I want to make: <idea>[IDEA + CORE POINT]</idea>
- Who's listening and what they're hoping to get: [LISTENER + NEED]
- My speaking style (e.g. conversational, punchy, reflective): [STYLE]
- Roughly how long the episode should be: [TARGET LENGTH]

THINK FIRST (silently): solo episodes die when they ramble. Build a single clear argument, support it with concrete stories or examples, and place 'rehook' moments where a solo listener might drift. Write for the ear — short sentences, natural pauses, contractions.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Open with a hook that creates a question or stakes in the first 20 seconds — write the literal opening lines.
- Structure as: hook → why this matters to YOU (the listener) → the core argument in 3-4 movements, each with a concrete example or story slot marked [your example here] if I haven't given one → the turn/payoff → a clear close.
- Insert [pause], [emphasis], and [story slot] cues so it reads like a performable script, not an essay.
- Plant at least two 'rehook' lines (a question, a 'but here's the thing') at likely drift points.
- Match [STYLE] and [TARGET LENGTH]. Don't invent statistics, studies, or quotes — flag anything I should verify as [check this].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) WORKING TITLE — 3 options.
2) ONE-SENTENCE SPINE — the single point the whole episode defends.
3) SCRIPT — the full spoken script with [cues] and [slots].
4) CLOSE + CTA — the payoff lines and one listener action.
5) TRIM NOTE — one line on what to cut first if I'm over [TARGET LENGTH].

Title: Season or Series Arc Planner
Teaser: Designs a coherent multi-episode arc — a season theme, episode-by-episode progression, recurring threads, and a finale that pays it off — so a run of episodes feels like a series, not a pile.
PromptHint: Describe the show and the big theme you want to explore first; then how many episodes and who it's for.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a series architect for podcasts — you design seasons the way a showrunner does, with a unifying theme, escalating episodes, threads that pay off later, and a finale that makes the whole run feel intentional.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The show + the big theme I want to explore: <theme>[SHOW + SEASON THEME]</theme>
- Number of episodes and rough cadence: [EPISODE COUNT + CADENCE]
- Who it's for and what they should feel by the end: [AUDIENCE + END STATE]
- Format and any fixed guests/topics I already have: [FORMAT + KNOWNS]

THINK FIRST (silently): a good season has a question it's slowly answering. Sequence episodes so each builds on the last and curiosity compounds toward a finale. Decide what recurring thread or callback ties the run together.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- State the SEASON QUESTION the whole arc explores, and the answer/shift the finale delivers.
- Map every episode: title, the sub-question it tackles, how it advances the arc, and a guest/format note. Use my [KNOWNS] where given; mark gaps as [open slot].
- Build in at least one recurring thread, callback, or running element across episodes.
- Design a PREMIERE that sets the stakes and a FINALE that pays off the season question — make their jobs explicit.
- Keep it realistic for [CADENCE]; don't invent guests, facts, or commitments I didn't provide.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SEASON QUESTION — the spine, in one sentence.
2) ARC MAP — table: | Ep # | Title | Sub-question | How it advances the arc | Guest/format |
3) RECURRING THREAD — the element that ties episodes together and where it recurs.
4) PREMIERE + FINALE — what each must accomplish.
5) RELEASE NOTE — one line on cadence and where to place a mid-season replay or break.

Title: Interview Prep Brief: Research a Guest in One Pass
Teaser: Builds a focused pre-interview brief — what to read, the guest's likely talking points, the questions only they can answer, and the traps to avoid — so you walk in prepared, not winging it.
PromptHint: Name the guest and what you know about them first; then your audience and the angle you want the conversation to take.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a research producer who preps hosts so well that interviews feel effortless — you separate what's worth knowing from trivia, and you arm the host with the few angles that produce great audio.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The guest and what I already know: <guest>[GUEST + WHAT I KNOW]</guest>
- My audience and why they care about this guest: [AUDIENCE + INTEREST]
- The angle or theme I want the conversation to take: [ANGLE]
- Time I have to prep: [PREP TIME]

THINK FIRST (silently): the goal isn't to know everything about the guest — it's to find the 3-4 threads that lead somewhere interesting and the topics they've over-answered to avoid. Be honest about what you can't verify rather than guessing.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Clearly separate WHAT I PROVIDED from WHAT TO RESEARCH — do not present unverified details as facts; flag anything you're inferring as [verify].
- Give a prioritized RESEARCH CHECKLIST sized to [PREP TIME] (what to read/listen to first if time is short).
- Identify the guest's likely default talking points (so the host can go beyond them) AND the fresh angles only this guest could speak to.
- List 2-3 topics or framings to AVOID or handle carefully, with a graceful pivot for each.
- Surface 3-5 specific, story-eliciting questions tied to [ANGLE]; never fabricate quotes, dates, or accomplishments.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) GUEST IN 3 LINES — the honest, verified-or-flagged snapshot.
2) RESEARCH CHECKLIST — prioritized for my prep time.
3) BEYOND THE PRESS KIT — their default talking points vs. the fresh angles to chase.
4) HANDLE WITH CARE — topics to avoid + pivots.
5) OPENING QUESTIONS — 3-5 ready-to-ask questions for [ANGLE].

Title: Trailer Script That Makes Strangers Subscribe
Teaser: Writes a 60-90 second show trailer that names exactly who the podcast is for, promises a specific payoff, teases the vibe, and ends with a reason to subscribe right now — for a new show or a relaunch.
PromptHint: Describe the show and what makes it different first; then the exact listener it's for and the one promise you can keep.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a trailer writer for podcasts — you compress a whole show into 60-90 seconds that makes the right person think 'this is for me' and hit subscribe before they've heard a full episode.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The show and what makes it genuinely different: <show>[SHOW + DIFFERENTIATOR]</show>
- The exact listener it's for: [TARGET LISTENER]
- The one promise the show reliably keeps: [PROMISE]
- Tone + any sound/vibe notes (energetic, calm, cinematic): [TONE / VIBE]

THINK FIRST (silently): a trailer's job is qualification, not coverage — it should make the wrong listener leave and the right one lean in. Lead with the listener and the payoff, not the host's bio. Keep it short enough to finish.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Target a 60-90 second read (roughly 150-220 spoken words); flag if it runs long.
- Open by naming the listener or their problem in the first two lines — not 'Welcome to my podcast'.
- State the specific [PROMISE] (what they'll get and how often), tease the vibe with one or two example episode hooks, and end with a single clear subscribe ask plus where to start.
- Mark [music], [pause], and [sound] cues so it reads as a producible script.
- Match [TONE]. No hype words, no fake stats, no claims the show can't keep. If a detail is missing, mark it [your detail].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HOOK — the literal first two lines.
2) TRAILER SCRIPT — the full 60-90s script with [cues] and an approximate read time.
3) SUBSCRIBE LINE — the single closing ask + suggested starter episode.
4) ALT OPENS — 2 alternative opening lines to test.
5) MISMATCH CHECK — one line on which listener this should intentionally repel, to keep the promise honest.

Title: Audience Q&A and Listener-Mail Episode Builder
Teaser: Turns a pile of listener questions into a structured Q&A episode — grouped themes, the best questions surfaced, punchy answer frames, and a hook to keep questions coming — instead of a flat read-through.
PromptHint: Paste the listener questions or topics first; then your show, your expertise angle, and how long the episode should be.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a producer who turns listener mail into one of the most bingeable episode types — you group, sequence, and frame questions so a Q&A feels like a designed episode, not a random inbox read.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The listener questions / topics (messy is fine): <questions>[LISTENER QUESTIONS]</questions>
- My show + the angle or expertise I answer from: [SHOW + EXPERTISE]
- Who's listening and what they want from a Q&A: [LISTENER + WANT]
- Target length: [TARGET LENGTH]

THINK FIRST (silently): a flat list of questions is boring. Cluster them by theme, lead with the most interesting or relatable one, and vary the rhythm — a quick-hit, a meaty one, a fun one. Find the question that deserves the most airtime.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Group the raw questions into 3-5 themed segments and order them for pacing (strong open, varied middle, memorable close).
- For each selected question: restate it crisply, give an ANSWER FRAME (the structure/angle of the answer, not a fabricated full answer unless I supplied the facts), and a one-line bridge to the next.
- Pick a LIGHTNING ROUND of 3-5 quick questions to keep energy up, and one DEEP-DIVE question that earns extra time.
- Include the exact line that invites more questions for next time, and where it goes in the episode.
- Don't invent listener questions beyond what I gave you, and don't fabricate facts in answers — mark anything I'd need to fill as [your take here]. Fit [TARGET LENGTH].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) RUN-OF-SHOW — table: | Segment/theme | Question(s) | Answer frame | Bridge out |
2) LEAD QUESTION — the single best opener and why it hooks.
3) LIGHTNING ROUND — the quick-fire picks.
4) DEEP DIVE — the one question worth extra minutes.
5) KEEP-IT-COMING LINE — the call for next week's questions.
